1:
The quiet opening word of the book — 'and it came to pass' — is itself, for the witnesses, a confession of continuity. Gill observes that the narrative begins 'as if something went before, it is connected with': it reads as a strict continuation of the last chapter of Deuteronomy, taking up the history at the very point where the account of Moses' death laid it down. Keil and Delitzsch add that the book takes its name not from its author but from its contents — the guiding of Israel into the land promised to the fathers — with vv.1–9 forming the introductory notice of the whole. Clarke contends at length that Joshua himself composed it, 'as truly his work as the Commentaries of Caesar are his,' against those who would assign it to Samuel or Ezra, allowing only that later copyists modernised a few place-names; and he lingers over the name Moses prophetically changed from Hoshea to Jehoshua, 'the salvation of Jehovah' (Numbers 13:16), the very name the Septuagint renders Jesus and the New Testament expressly gives him in Acts 7:45 and Hebrews 4:8.

On the time and manner of the call the witnesses fill out the terse notice. Wesley takes 'after the death of Moses' to mean either immediately after it or when the days of mourning were expired, Joshua having been appointed successor before this and here receiving confirmation from God. Henry is more definite: an interregnum even of a few days might have been of bad consequence, yet God probably waited until the thirty days of mourning had ended — not because grief unfitted Joshua for communion with God, but so that by this solemn pause God 'would put an honour upon the memory of Moses' and give the people time to repent of their miscarriages towards him. As for the manner, Henry thinks God spoke as he had spoken to Moses out of the tabernacle (Leviticus 1:1), where Joshua had presented himself with him (Deuteronomy 31:14), or perhaps in a dream or vision (Job 33:15); Wesley adds the possibility of the Urim (Numbers 27:21).

The two titles of the verse carry its theology. 'The servant of the LORD' is Moses' epitaph, and Wesley draws a striking inference: the title honours Moses and gives authority to his writings, yet reminds Israel 'that he was not the Lord himself, but only the Lord's servant,' whose institutions must one day give place 'to him who was the son, and heir, and Lord' of the house. 'Moses' minister' is Joshua's apprenticeship: Henry notes that he who was called to honour had long been bred to business, for 'those are fittest to rule that have learnt to obey,' and sees in the servant exalted a type of Christ. Ellicott finds here something without precedent: no man before Joshua had received orders to regulate his conduct by a written book — Abraham and Moses heard God's own mouth, but Joshua and every successor after him 'must fulfil the orders of this book of the law,' so that he was Moses' minister twice over, his attendant in life and the executor of what he wrote. Calvin gathers it up: the renewed commission displays God's steadfastness in watching over his people, that they might not think themselves forsaken by Moses' death, and was given less for Joshua's sake than to embolden a people who saw their leader advance not a single step until the Lord had preceded him.
2:
'Moses my servant is dead' — the witnesses agree that the sentence is no obituary. Gill says it was spoken 'not for the information of Joshua,' who had helped bury his master, but to lead on to and give the reason for the command that follows. Calvin weighs two possible senses — 'since Moses is dead, the whole burden has now devolved upon thee,' or 'although Moses is dead, do not desist, but go forward' — and prefers the former: Joshua is to take up, by right of succession, the office Moses had left vacant. He adds that the epithet 'servant' here respects Moses' leadership rather than his lawgiving, and is spoken 'not so much with the view of praising him, as of strengthening the authority of Joshua, who had been substituted in his place'; and he asks why God appeals to the word given to Moses rather than the older oath to Abraham, answering that Moses was 'a witness whose memory was more recent,' lest the people's confidence be shaken by his death.

Clarke dwells on the title itself: 'servant,' applied to Moses and Joshua, bears a very peculiar sense — God's prime minister, the person by whom he issued his orders and accomplished his designs — and 'no person ever bore this title in the like sense but the Redeemer of mankind, of whom Moses and Joshua were types.' The same evangelical instinct animates Wesley, who turns the death-notice into a maxim for every generation: 'Moses the servant is dead; but God the master is not: he lives for ever.' Let not the withering of the most useful hands be the weakening of ours, for when God has work to do, he will either find or make instruments fit to carry it on.

The command and the gift are then read together. Keil and Delitzsch state the plain sense: Moses had died without bringing Israel into Canaan, so Joshua must arise and cross 'this Jordan' — the river then before him — with all the nation, into the land the Lord was giving them. Wesley notes that 'this Jordan' was now the only obstacle in the way to Canaan, and that 'which I give' means 'am now about to give thee actual possession of it, as I formerly gave a right to it by promise.' Clarke, ever the antiquarian, even supplies Josephus's account of the river's hidden source at Phiala, traced by chaff thrown into the pool and recovered at Panium. But the pastoral centre is Calvin's: because 'nothing tends more than distrust to make us sluggish and useless,' God does not merely order the crossing but holds forth a happy issue, promising peaceable possession of every spot on which they should plant their foot — 'when God holds forth a happy issue, confidence inspires us with vigour for any attempt.' The work outlives the workman because the Master outlives them both.
3:
The grammarians fasten on the tense. Keil and Delitzsch observe that 'every place that the sole of your foot shall tread upon' means the whole land, 'not excepting a single foot's breadth,' and that the perfect — 'that have I given unto you' — 'refers to the counsel of God as having been formed long before, and being now about to be carried into execution.' The words echo Deuteronomy 11:24 in form and rest upon the promise of Exodus 23:30–31, to which 'as I said unto Moses' points back. The gift is thus older than the conquest: Israel does not win the land and then receive it; she receives it and then walks onto it. Gill confines the sweep to the land of Canaan itself, and Wesley likewise reads 'every place' as 'within the following bounds' of v.4.

Clarke draws from the figure a note of ease: the words were 'intended to express the ease with which they were to conquer the whole land, an instance of which occurs in the taking of Jericho' — as though possession asked no more than a footstep. 'It was only their unfaithfulness to God that rendered the conquest in any case difficult.' The promise, in other words, was never the narrow thing their history made of it; the shrinkage lay in the treaders, not in the grant.

Ellicott supplies the careful qualification that keeps the promise honest against the record of the book. The conquest as Joshua effected it 'was a limited conquest': he took a certain number of strongholds, utterly crushed the armies opposed to him in the field, established Israel in the position he had won, and then divided to the tribes 'the whole territory, conquered and unconquered alike' (Joshua 13:1–7) — the Philistines and Sidonians being examples of nations never subdued by him yet assigned to Israel for an inheritance. Hence his fine epigram on this verse: 'what Israel would conquer, the sole of his foot must tread.' The conquest Joshua began for the nation must be carried out in detail by the several tribes themselves. Read together, the witnesses turn the verse into a parable of promise and appropriation: God's gift is complete, formed in his counsel before a sword was drawn, yet it becomes enjoyment only step by step, at the pace of obedient feet. What Clarke states negatively — only unfaithfulness made it hard — Ellicott states positively: the grant covered far more than was ever grasped, and the difference between deed and possession was always the measure of Israel's faith, as the later chapters of the book, and the witnesses on v.4, go on to show.
4:
The witnesses first walk the boundaries. Keil and Delitzsch note that the limits are those of Deuteronomy 11:24: from the desert of Arabia in the south and Lebanon in the north, eastward to 'the great river, the river Euphrates,' and westward to 'the great sea toward the going down of the sun,' the Mediterranean, with 'all the land of the Hittites' inserted between the two termini. Clarke pictures Joshua 'standing with his face towards the promised land, and pointing out the different places with his hand — this Lebanon'; and he explains 'the great sea' as great in comparison with Gennesareth and the Dead Sea, since the Hebrews called every large collection of waters a sea. On the Hittites the witnesses part company: Clarke takes them as the most hardy and warlike of the Canaanitish nations, named as the hardest case but standing for all the rest, and Wesley agrees that the one formidable tribe here represents the whole, as 'Amorites' does elsewhere (Genesis 15:16); but Ellicott thinks it better to understand 'the land of the Hittites' of the northern districts about Hamath and Carchemish, between Palestine proper and the Euphrates.

The pressing difficulty is that Israel never held all this. Clarke concedes the nation 'did not possess the full extent of this grant till the days of David' (2 Samuel 8:3; 2 Chronicles 9:26). Wesley meets the objection head-on: 'That was from their own sloth and cowardice, and disobedience to God, and breach of those conditions upon which this promise was suspended' — adding that though their possessions never reached the Euphrates, their dominion did, all those lands paying tribute under David and Solomon. Calvin argues the same at greater depth: the promise was 'by no means broken or rendered void by the delay'; the people had it in their power to fill the prescribed boundaries in due time and 'they declined to do so,' deserving for that to have been expelled altogether, while the divine indulgence still granted territory sufficient for their commodious habitation.

Henry, surveying the same grant, draws the widest circle: had they been obedient, God would have given them this and much more; and if the promise had not its full accomplishment in the letter, believers might infer 'that it had a further meaning, and was to be fulfilled in the kingdom of the Messiah, both that of grace and that of glory.' Calvin's editors record the kindred suggestion that the very vastness and indefiniteness of the boundaries was designed to elevate the minds of Old Testament believers beyond the present, to a period when, under a new and more glorious dispensation, the promise would be completely fulfilled. So the witnesses leave the map open at both edges: a real geography forfeited in part by unbelief, and a larger inheritance — the true bounds of the true Joshua's kingdom — of which these borders were the pledge.
5:
What was promised to the nation in common is here made personal to its captain. Gill notes that the assurance 'there shall not any man be able to stand before thee' is spoken particularly to Joshua the general, was fulfilled in him, 'and still more in Christ his antitype,' who made an end of sin, spoiled principalities and powers, abolished death, and overcame the world. Clarke is content to observe that the promise 'was most punctually and literally fulfilled' — no enemy ever finally stood before Joshua all his days.

Calvin explains why so absolute a pledge was needed: a contest was about to be waged with numerous and warlike enemies, and without this the promise of the land 'would ever and anon have become darkened; for how vast the enterprise, to overthrow so many nations!' Therefore God points Joshua back to the victories of Moses, that he might 'behold in the assistance given to Moses the future issue of the wars' he was now to undertake — 'for the series of favours is continued without interruption to the successor.' Henry turns the same clause toward every rising generation: Joshua knew how far he came short of Moses in wisdom and grace, but what Moses did was done by the presence of God with him, and if Joshua had the same presence he 'would do well enough' — the grace sufficient for those who went before shall not be wanting to those who follow, if they are not wanting to themselves. Wesley distributes the promise into its two gifts: God would leave him destitute 'neither of inward support, nor of outward assistance.'

On 'I will not fail thee, nor forsake thee' the witnesses converge on the apostolic use of the words. Ellicott traces the pedigree: compare Genesis 28:15 to Jacob, and consider Hebrews 13:5 'as a combination of the two Old Testament passages.' Calvin presses the striking fact that the apostle there applies a war-charter to the sin of avarice: since 'the distrust which arises from anxiety kindles in us such tumultuous feelings' that at the least appearance of danger we turmoil and miserably torment ourselves, God 'prescribes no other cure for our timidity' than the assurance of his present aid — enough, and more than enough, whether the enemy is a walled city or tomorrow's bread. Henry adds that the promise made to Joshua is thereby 'applied to all believers,' and coins the balancing maxim: 'the Lord is with us while we are with him.' There is no standing before those who have God on their side — if he be for us, who can be against us (Romans 8:31)? — and there is no ground of confidence anywhere else; the whole conquest, and the whole Christian life, hangs on the unwearied 'I will be with thee.'
6:
'Be strong and of a good courage' now sounds for the first of three times in this charge, and the witnesses ask why a proven soldier needed it. Gill recalls that Moses had already given Joshua the same exhortation, and that it is repeated in this chapter 'as being of great moment and importance,' since it belongs to the general of an army to show greatness and strength of mind and not be dismayed at the number and power of the enemy; and he cannot forbear his customary glance forward — as Joshua's work of conquering Canaan required strength and courage, 'so Christ's work in the redemption of his people, and subduing their enemies,' required the same, 'and both were very eminent in him.' Wesley answers from Joshua's circumstances: he needed the exhortation partly because his work was 'great, and difficult, and long, and in a great measure new,' and partly because 'he had a very mean opinion of himself,' especially compared with Moses, and remembering how perverse and ungovernable the people had been even under Moses, might well suspect the burden would prove too heavy for his shoulders.

Calvin draws the doctrine of the repetition. The exhortation is 'added, and indeed repeated, that it may make the deeper impression'; for although it is the property of faith to animate us to strenuous exertion, 'bare promises are not sufficiently energetic without the additional stimulus of exhortation.' Then comes his searching a-fortiori: 'if Joshua, who was always remarkable for alacrity, required to be incited to the performance of duty, how much more necessary must it be that we, who labour under so much sluggishness, should be spurred forward.' Nor is Joshua confirmed once only: strength and constancy are pressed on him 'repeatedly and in various terms, because he was to be engaged in many and various contests' — it would not have been enough to begin briskly without being prepared to persevere in the struggle.

The ground of the courage is the clause the verse attaches to it: 'for unto this people shalt thou divide for an inheritance the land which I sware unto their fathers.' Keil and Delitzsch hear in 'thou shalt divide' an echo of the commissions of Deuteronomy 1:38 and Deuteronomy 3:28 — Joshua is to rely firmly on the Lord and his promise, as Moses and the Lord had already told him. Henry finds here a double encouragement: it was a great encouragement, beginning the work, to be assured he would live to see it finished, so that his labour should not be in vain; though some, he notes wryly, make the character of the people themselves the reason for the charge — Joshua knew what a froward, discontented people he must cause to inherit the land, 'let him therefore expect vexation from them, and be of good courage.' Courage, the witnesses agree, is not a temperament but an obedience, resting on an oath sworn to the fathers and certain of its end.
7:
The second 'be strong' surprises the witnesses by its object: not the Canaanites now, but the law. Keil and Delitzsch mark the movement of the charge — in v.6 Joshua was to be firm by relying on the Lord's promise; here 'he was to strive to attain and preserve this firmness by a careful observance of the law,' turning from it neither to the right hand nor to the left. Courage of the sword, the sequence implies, is kept alive by courage of obedience. Gill adds the military reason for the renewed charge: Joshua was to engage a people more and mightier than Israel, dwelling in strong and fortified places, forewarned and long preparing for the assault; valour proved against Amalek still needed confirming against such odds.

Clarke relishes the Septuagint's rendering — 'be strong therefore, and play the man to the uttermost' — and draws from the verse his characteristic doctrine of means: though God had promised that no man should be able to stand before him, 'yet it was on condition that he should use all his military skill, and avail himself to the uttermost of all the means, natural and providential, which God should place within his reach. God will not have them who refuse to help themselves.' Wesley reads the same condition from the side of authority: 'Remember, that though thou art the commander of my people, yet thou art my subject' — and the prohibition of turning aside 'in any kind, or upon any pretence... plainly shews, that God's assistance promised to him and the Israelites was conditional, and might justly be withdrawn upon their breach of the conditions.' His gloss on 'whithersoever thou goest' widens it to 'whatsoever thou doest,' men's actions being ways by which they come to the end they aim at.

Henry states the constitutional principle: God, as it were, puts the book of the law into Joshua's hand, as the testimony was given to the young king Joash at his crowning (2 Kings 11:12); and Joshua, a man of great power and authority, 'must himself be under command and do as he is bidden. No man's dignity or dominion, how great soever, sets him above the law of God.' He must govern by the law, observe it himself, and by his own example maintain its honour. Ellicott adds two quieter observations: the command so often repeated 'may perhaps be taken as reflecting light upon his natural character,' which might not of itself have coveted so prominent a position; and courage was especially needed to carry out the conquest in the manner the law prescribed — the sternest part of Joshua's obedience. The promise annexed seals it all: do according to all the law, and 'thou shalt prosper' — or, as Henry notes from the margin, 'do wisely'; for the witnesses agree that in Scripture the truly prudent man and the truly obedient man are the same man.
8:
'This book of the law shall not depart out of thy mouth.' Gill takes the plain sense — Joshua was often to read it, frequently repeat it, and speak of it, refreshing his own memory and the memory of those about him — and reports Jarchi's opinion that the book in his hand was Deuteronomy. Clarke observes that though a copy of the law lay in the sanctuary, 'this was not sufficient. Joshua must have a copy for himself, and he was to consult it incessantly'; and he draws the covenant logic of the verse: if he kept God's word, God would keep him; 'those who are obedient to God lack no manner of thing that is good.'

Calvin pauses over the choice of organ: why must the law not depart from his mouth rather than from his eyes? Some call it a synecdoche for the whole man, 'but this is frigid.' The word fits one who was bound to prosecute the study 'not only for himself individually, but for the whole people placed under his rule': Joshua must bring forward what he has learned for the common benefit, teaching others while making his own conduct conformable to the same rule — for 'many, by talking and discoursing, have the law in their mouth, but are very bad keepers of it.' Meditation must also be assiduous, since whenever it is intermitted 'the memory becomes rusted'; God enjoins his servant to make daily progress his whole life long, and 'those who hold this study in disdain are blinded by intolerable arrogance.' The promise attached cuts both ways: whatever profane men attempt in contempt of the word of God 'must ultimately fail of success,' however prosperous its commencement.

Ellicott hears in the verse the fountain of a psalm: the words are 'taken up again in Psalms 1:2–3, and a blessing is pronounced on every man who takes Joshua's position in relation to the written law of God.' Here the difference between Moses and all who follow him appears: Moses was the prophet whom the Lord knew face to face; 'Joshua and all his successors, from the least to the greatest, find their blessing and their portion in the careful study and fulfilment of the written word.' And from its first appearance as a book, that word 'is supreme. It is set above Joshua. It is never superseded' — its authority independent of its quantity, whether 'the law of Moses' or the whole finished canon. Wesley and Henry press the practical corner: the greatness of Joshua's place and employments could not excuse him from this work, since the law was the rule of all his private actions and public administrations; indeed, says Henry, 'if ever any man's business might have excused him from meditation, one would think Joshua's might at this time' — yet he must find time and thoughts for it, for whatever affairs of this world we have to mind, 'we must not neglect the one thing needful.' Prosperity and good success, the witnesses conclude, are promised not to the busy hand but to the murmuring mouth and the meditating heart.
9:
The charge closes not with a new command but with a question that gathers all the rest into it: 'Have not I commanded thee?' Gill bids the reader weigh the speaker: the orders to cross the Jordan, to keep the law of Moses, to be strong and of good courage, come from 'the great Jehovah, the everlasting I AM, who is faithful to his promises, and able to perform.' The consideration of who commands, he says, would animate Joshua to the work, encourage his faith, and engage him to serve cheerfully and readily. Calvin attends to the grammar of the question: in Hebrew a simple affirmation often takes the interrogative form, 'here, however, the question is emphatic' — God brings his own authority distinctly forward to relieve his servant from care and hesitancy. And from it he draws one of his surest counsels: 'nothing is more effectual to produce confidence than when, trusting to the call and the command of God, and feeling fully assured of it in our own conscience, we follow whithersoever he is pleased to lead.'

Wesley unfolds the divine 'I' into three assurances: 'I whom thou art obliged to obey: I who can carry thee through every thing I put thee upon: I of whose faithfulness and almighty power thou hast had large experience.' Obligation, sufficiency, and experience — duty rests on authority, but courage rests on memory. Henry hears the same double force: 'I have commanded the work to be done, and therefore it shall be done, how invincible soever the difficulties'; and 'I have commanded, called, and commissioned thee to do it, and therefore will be sure to own thee, and strengthen thee, and bear thee out in it.' When we are in the way of our duty, it will embolden us to keep our eye on the divine warrant, hearing God say, 'Have not I commanded thee? I will therefore help thee, succeed thee, accept thee, reward thee.' He even carries the pattern to its highest instance: our Lord Jesus, as Joshua here, was borne up under his sufferings by the commandment he had received from his Father (John 10:18).

So the verse ends where the paragraph began at v.5, with presence: 'for the LORD thy God is with thee whithersoever thou goest.' Henry observes that those who go where God sends them shall have him with them wherever they go, 'and they need desire no more to make them easy and prosperous.' The witnesses read the threefold 'be strong' as answered by a threefold ground — the command of God, the call of God, the company of God — and find in the question-form itself the cure for dismay: fear interrogated is fear dissolved, for the man who can answer 'yes, he has commanded me' has nothing left to be afraid of.
10:
With the divine charge received, 'then Joshua commanded the officers of the people' — and Henry savours the immediacy: settled in the government, Joshua 'immediately applies himself to business; not to take state or to take his pleasure,' for he that desires office desires work, and none is preferred in God's kingdom in order to be idle. The officers themselves draw his eye: inferior magistrates are as necessary and serviceable to the public good in their places as the supreme magistrate in his — 'what could Joshua have done without officers?' — a hierarchy he finds still binding in 1 Peter 2:13–14.

Who these shoterim were, the witnesses debate. Clarke distinguishes them from the shophetim, the judges who determined civil causes, and reports Calmet's conjecture that they were 'the heralds of the army,' like those in Homer who bore sceptres and whose persons were held sacred. Gill canvasses the options — elsewhere the word designates attendants who executed the judges' orders, but here, he thinks, rather captains and officers of the army, unless a sort of heralds making proclamation through the camp. Keil and Delitzsch give the more prosaic account: the shoterim were the keepers of the family registers, whose duty embraced both the levying of men bound to serve and the circulation of the general's commands — the administrative nervous system of the host. Wesley is content that they commanded under Joshua in their tribes and families, attending him for orders which they transmitted to the people.

Behind the order lies a question of chronology which Calvin presses with conviction: he is 'fully convinced' that this proclamation was issued only after the spies of the second chapter had returned, since it would have been 'preposterous haste to hurry on an unknown path' before he was informed; the story of Rahab must therefore be read as 'a kind of interposed parenthesis,' Scripture often neglecting the order of time and afterwards interweaving what had been omitted. Henry supposes the same. And Calvin finds in the confident date a miracle of faith: Joshua declares the people will pass Jordan within three days, though no one had attempted the ford, there was no bridge or boats, and nothing was easier for the enemy than to prevent the passage — 'this he never would have ventured to do, without the suggestion of the Spirit'; the only thing that remained 'was for God to transport them miraculously,' and the people's prompt obedience showed that they had cast their care upon God. Ellicott adds a structural observation: the crossing stands to Joshua as Sinai stood to Moses — each preceded by three days' notice and a sanctifying of the people, each God's chosen means 'to establish the leaders whom He had chosen' (compare Exodus 19:9–11 with Joshua 3:7 and Joshua 4:14). Command from God becomes command through men: the first act of the new captain is to set the whole ordered body of Israel in motion on the strength of a bare promise.
11:
'Prepare you victuals' — a homely order, but it opens a genuine debate among the witnesses, for what of the manna? Keil and Delitzsch answer flatly that the provision meant is 'not manna, for that had already ceased,' but the natural produce of the now inhabited country. Ellicott takes the opposite view: the manna 'did not cease until several days after they passed the Jordan' (Joshua 5:12), yet no other meaning can be assigned to the word than provision for a journey or a warlike expedition; perhaps the order was meant to prepare Israel for the transition from manna to common food — when there was difficulty in obtaining provision God gave manna, but 'now, when they could easily provide food for themselves, He would not support them in idleness.' Wesley mediates: manna supplied their want in the wilderness, but they were always allowed to purchase other provisions when they could (Deuteronomy 2:6), and having now been some time in the Amorites' fertile land they had grown used to other food, with which they must furnish themselves for the march. Clarke notes that the word tsedah properly signifies prey — corn, oxen, and sheep taken from the conquered countries; and Henry adds the touch the others leave implicit: Joshua bids them prepare victuals, 'not to prepare transport vessels' — he that bore them out of Egypt upon eagles' wings (Exodus 19:4) would in like manner bear them into Canaan. He even reports the Jewish writers who, considering that manna still fell, take the command figuratively: repent, make your peace with God, resolve on a new life, that you may be ready for so great a favour.

The 'three days' raise their own question, since the spies' errand intervenes. Clarke follows Calmet: the days are reckoned from the encampment at Jordan, three days after the spies returned, the river being crossed on the tenth day of the month. Wesley prefers the simpler solution that these words, 'though placed here, seem not to have been delivered by Joshua till after the return of the spies, such transpositions being frequent in scripture' — hence the same three days reappear at Joshua 3:2. Either way the order stands on the same faith Calvin admired in v.10: a date fixed for a crossing that only God could effect.

The ground given to the people repeats the divine grant: ye shall pass over 'to possess the land which the LORD your God giveth you to possess it.' Henry marks 'with what assurance Joshua says to the people, because God had said it to him, You shall pass over Jordan, and shall possess the land' — and turns it into the rule of all preaching: 'we greatly honour the truth of God when we stagger not at the promise of God.' Provision in the hand and promise in the ear: with these two the witnesses see Israel made ready for the impossible river.
12:
Joshua's next word is not to the host at large but to Reuben, Gad, and the half of Manasseh — the tribes, as Gill notes, already 'settled on that side Jordan where Israel now were,' whose case was therefore unlike every other. Ellicott points to the history that explains the address: the covenant of Numbers 32, by which Moses granted them Gilead and Bashan on the express condition of armed service across the river; and he adds the practical mitigation, visible at Joshua 4:12, that they 'were not forbidden to leave a sufficient number of their fighting men to protect their homes and families.'

Calvin gives the fullest analysis, and finds Joshua pleading by two arguments, 'the one drawn from authority and the other from equity.' From authority: he reminds them of the command of Moses, 'from whose decision it was not lawful to deviate, since it was well known to all that he uttered nothing of himself, but only what God had dictated by his mouth' — and at the same time, without asserting it in words, 'indirectly insinuates that they are bound by compact,' having themselves engaged to go armed before the Lord until the land was subdued. From equity: 'it would be very incongruous, he says, that your brethren should be incurring danger, or at least toiling in carrying on war, and that you should be enjoying all the comforts of a peaceful settlement.' A common inheritance forbids an unequal burden; those first served must not be first seated.

Calvin also guards the phrase 'pass before your brethren' from a martial misreading: the meaning is not that the two tribes and a half were to stand in the front of every battle and bear more than their share, 'he only in this way urges them to move with alacrity, as it would have been a kind of tergiversation to keep in the rear and follow slowly in the track of others' — they must keep their ranks and give proof of ready zeal. And from the term 'men of war' he infers a humane arrangement: the aged and those not robust 'were permitted to remain at home in charge of the common welfare,' or relieved altogether if disabled. The verse thus becomes, in the witnesses' hands, a small charter of covenanted mutuality: privileges taken early are mortgaged to the service of brethren whose portion is still unwon, and the God who dictated the bargain through Moses holds his people to it through Joshua. What Moses commanded and they promised, death has not dissolved; the obligations of God's house, like its promises, pass intact from one administration to the next.
13:
'Remember the word which Moses the servant of the LORD commanded you.' Gill notes that Joshua appeals to an agreement 'to which they had solemnly agreed,' and now that the time had come to execute it, simply reminds them of it; Clarke likewise points back to the engagements made 'when he granted them their portion on the east side of Jordan' (Numbers 32:20). Wesley compresses the appeal into a pair: 'his charge to you, and your promise to him.' Keil and Delitzsch add a fine literary observation: in recalling the condition, Joshua follows the wording of Deuteronomy 3:18–20, where Moses himself recapitulated his former command, rather than the original narrative of Numbers 32 — the new leader quotes his master's own last form of the charge.

It is the phrase 'the LORD your God hath given you rest' that draws out the richest comment. Ellicott bids us observe it closely: on both sides of Jordan equally there was 'the rest of God,' so that those who condemn the two tribes and a half — or whomever they are made to represent spiritually — 'for not going far enough' should take notice. Yet this rest is 'only the first stage of several in Israel's history': it recurs under David (2 Samuel 7:1) and Solomon (1 Chronicles 22:9), and the argument of Hebrews 4, drawn from Psalms 95, closes the series: 'if Joshua had given them rest, then would He not afterward have spoken of another day... There remaineth therefore a rest to the people of God.' His conclusion has the whole book in it: 'the last rest is Sabbatical; the rests that precede it are halting-places on the way.'

Henry converts the doctrine into duty. The Lord hath given you rest — rest of mind, for they knew what they had to trust to while their brethren still waited on the issue of war and lot; rest for their families, safely settled in a good land. 'When God by his providence has given us rest, we ought to consider how we may honour him with the advantages of it, and what service we may do to our brethren who are unsettled, or not so well settled as we are.' His example is David, who, when God had given him rest (2 Samuel 7:1), was restless until he had found a habitation for the ark (Psalms 132:4–5); his warning, that when God has given us rest 'we must take heed of slothfulness and of settling upon our lees.' So the witnesses read the verse twice over: as covenant memory — a promise made to Moses now falling due — and as a standing parable, that every rest God gives short of the last one is given not for retirement but for service.
14:
The terms of the arrangement are set out with the precision of a muster-roll, and the witnesses supply the arithmetic. Wives, little ones, and cattle are to remain in the land Moses gave — and Gill remarks that this was no hardship imposed but 'what they themselves proposed,' agreed to, and confirmed in the covenant of Numbers 32:16–27. Clarke works out the numbers: comparing Numbers 32:17 with Joshua 4:13, it appears that only about forty thousand of the two tribes and a half passed over armed to assist their brethren, while some seventy thousand effective men were left behind to guard the families and property. On the word rendered 'armed' — chamushim, 'by fives' — he suggests men marching in ranks five abreast, or more generally 'arrayed, equipped, accoutred, well-armed, and ready for battle' (Exodus 13:18).

Wesley justifies the order of march. That they should pass over 'before their brethren' — in the front of them all — 'was but reasonable': they had the advantage of their brethren, 'having actually received their portion, which their brethren had only in hope'; they were free from the impediments the rest carried, their wives, children, and estates being safely lodged; and marching in the van would prevent their withdrawing from the service, 'which they otherwise would have had temptation to do, because of the nearness of their habitations.' He also explains the selection: all the mighty men of valour were obliged to go if occasion required, but Joshua took only a part, both because they sufficed and because some were fit to be left to secure their own families and to keep the enemies on that side from disturbing the enterprise — the same point Ellicott makes on v.12.

Henry names the moral cost that the logistics conceal: interest would make the other tribes glad to go over Jordan, 'but in these it was an act of self-denial, and against the grain,' since they left their possessions behind and fought for an inheritance that would never be theirs. That is why the agreement made with Moses had to be produced and pressed. Calvin's word on the previous verse governs this one too: equity forbids that some toil while others of the same household rest. The witnesses thus find in the marching order a picture of the communion of God's people: those who are settled first owe their strength to the unsettled, the strongest place in the column belongs to those with the least left to lose, and no tribe's rest is finished while a brother's war goes on.
15:
The witnesses' comment on this verse is brief, but the verse itself carries the whole covenant logic of the passage, and what they do say points at its heart. Gill glosses 'until the LORD have given your brethren rest, as he hath given you' as 'rest from their travels, as they had' — the eastern tribes had already come to the end of their forty years' wandering, and the service required of them is measured exactly by the mercy they had received: as he hath given you. Their discharge is dated not by a term of years but by a brother's blessing; they are bound to the war until every tribe can say of its own portion what they can already say of theirs. Clarke confines himself to the compass-points: 'toward the sun-rising' is the east, as 'toward the going down of the sun' in v.4 is the west — the sacred writer fixing the transjordanic inheritance by the same simple reckoning with which he had drawn the bounds of Canaan itself.

The verse's own words repay the drawing out. Twice in one sentence it is said that Moses 'gave' them the land — 'the land of your possession, which Moses the LORD's servant gave you' — as if to assure the departing soldiers that their title deed does not lapse while they are away: the service across Jordan is not the price of the grant but its condition of honour, and when the work is done they shall not merely return to it but 'enjoy' it, possess it with a clear conscience and an unmortgaged title. The promise 'then ye shall return' makes the campaign a parenthesis, not a forfeiture.

And the little clause 'as he hath given you' quietly universalises the verse. The rest already enjoyed east of Jordan and the rest still to be won west of it are the same rest, given by the same Lord — the observation Ellicott presses on v.13, where he traces the word through David and Solomon to the argument of Hebrews 4:8, that a greater rest remained beyond anything Joshua secured. Read in that light, the verse is a miniature of the church militant: some of God's people are, as it were, already settled, and some still under arms; but none is permitted to enjoy its rest as a private possession until the Lord has given rest to all, and the final enjoyment — the return of every warrior to an inheritance kept safe for him — waits for the day when the last brother's warfare is accomplished.
16:
'And they answered Joshua, saying, All that thou commandest us we will do.' Who answers? Gill takes the speakers to be the two tribes and a half just addressed, their deputed heads being 'their mouths to him.' Henry reads more widely: the answer 'was given not by the two tribes and a half only... but by the officers of all the people, as their representatives, concurring with the divine appointment by which Joshua was set over them, and they did it heartily, and with a great deal of cheerfulness and resolution.' Ellicott and Wesley agree that this is the whole people's reply — Wesley adding that in it they concurred 'with the divine appointment,' so that the voice of Israel seconds the voice of God.

The substance of the pledge is soldierly. Henry hears in it the centurion's grammar — he that hath soldiers under him saith to this man, Go, and he goeth (Matthew 8:9) — obedience promised 'not only as subjects to their prince, but as soldiers to their general,' without murmuring or disputing, on the most difficult and perilous expedition; and he makes the application the church has always made: 'thus must we swear allegiance to our Lord Jesus, as the captain of our salvation,' bound to do what he commands by his word and go where he sends by his providence. Clarke notes simply that in these words 'they acknowledge the Divine mission of Joshua, as they had done that of Moses.'

Calvin defends the answer against a charge of immodesty. They 'not only acquiesce, but freely admit and explicitly detail the obedience which they owe' — and our obligations, he observes, are duly discharged 'only when we perform them cheerfully, and not in sadness,' as Paul says of the giver in 2 Corinthians 9:7. But is there not little modesty in professing to have obeyed Moses, whom Israel so often contradicted? Calvin answers that these were not the rebellious spirits of whom God complains in Psalms 95:8–11, but a generation 'subdued by the examples of punishment,' who had learned quietly to submit; and the words in any case are spoken 'not so much to herald their own virtues as to extol the authority of Joshua,' whom they engage to regard exactly as they regarded Moses. Henry handles the same difficulty more bluntly — 'to speak truth, they had no reason to boast of their obedience to Moses' — and takes them to mean that they would be as observant of Joshua as they should have been of his master; whence his golden rule for every succession: 'we must not so magnify those that are gone... as to be wanting in the honour and duty we owe to those that survive and succeed them, though in gifts they may come short of them.' Ellicott crowns the scene with the observation that the people end, at v.18, by repeating to Joshua the very words of Jehovah — 'be strong and of a good courage' — the congregation echoing back to its leader the encouragement of God himself.
17:
The people bind their future to their past: 'according as we hearkened unto Moses in all things, so will we hearken unto thee.' Gill supplies the necessary limit within the sweeping phrase: they promise to hearken 'not right or wrong, but in all things that were according to the laws and will of God made known to them' — and particularly, he thinks, in the matter just settled, the eastern tribes confirming to Joshua the agreement they had kept with Moses. Wesley reads it as a simple transfer of allegiance: 'the same obedience which we owed to Moses, we promise unto thee.' The succession is complete when the people, and not only God, say so.

The delicate clause is the next one: 'only the LORD thy God be with thee, as he was with Moses.' Is this a proviso or a prayer? Clarke allows both readings: taken one way it is a condition — 'provided God be with thee, as he was with Moses, we will implicitly obey thee' — but the words 'may mean no more than an earnest prayer for Joshua's prosperity: May God be with thee, as he was with Moses!' Wesley rules for the prayer: 'this is not a limitation of their obedience, as if they would not obey him any longer than he was prosperous, but an additional prayer for him' — as we have promised thee our obedience, so we shall pray that God would bless and prosper thee as he did Moses. Henry reports the restrictive reading — some understand it, 'we will obey only as far as we perceive the Lord is with thee; while thou keepest close to God we will keep close to thee' — but judges that the people 'were so far from having any suspicion of Joshua's deviating from the divine rule that there needed not such a proviso.' For him the clause is intercession, and a pattern of it: prayers are to be made for all in authority (1 Timothy 2:1–2), and 'the best thing we can ask of God for our magistrates is that they may have the presence of God with them; this will make them blessings to us, so that in seeking this for them we consult our own interest.'

Calvin, treating the answer as a whole, catches the tone exactly: 'the form of expression is intermediate between the confidence of faith and prayer' — they intimate the good hope they cherish, and at the same time have recourse to prayer, 'under a conviction of the arduousness of the work'; and the epithet 'thy God' is not without weight, 'as it evidently points to a continued course of divine favour.' On any reading the witnesses converge here: the people's obedience to the new leader rests, as his own courage does, on one thing only — that the God of Moses go with him.
18:
The people's answer ends with an edge of iron: 'whosoever he be that doth rebel against thy commandment... he shall be put to death.' Gill identifies the rebel in view — whoever refuses to go over Jordan with his brethren when Joshua commands. Clarke names the category: 'this was martial law,' the death of the disobedient soldier, and essentially necessary for discipline in so great an army. Both he and Henry read it against the shameful record of the last generation: by insubordination their fathers had suffered much in the wilderness — they rejected the authority of Moses, mutinied, and appointed themselves a captain to return to Egypt (Numbers 14:4), and Joshua himself, for encouraging them against their fears, was nearly stoned. Hence, says Clarke, the most positive assurance now that they would not act as their fathers had done. Henry, with his dry realism, adds that had such a law been made in Moses' time 'it might have prevented many of the rebellions that were formed against him; for most men fear the sword of the magistrate more than the justice of God' — and in times of war the severity of military discipline is more necessary than at other times. He also reports those who hear in the statute an echo of the prophet like unto Moses (Deuteronomy 18:19), whose word, refused, brings a reckoning — chiefly Christ, with Joshua by the way as his type.

Yet the sentence closes not with a threat but with a benediction turned back upon the leader: 'only be strong and of a good courage.' Henry is charmed by the reversal — Joshua, though of approved valour, 'did not take it as an affront, but as a great kindness, for the people to bid him be strong'; in desiring it they in effect promised to encourage him by an exact, bold, and cheerful observance of all his orders, for 'it very much heartens those that lead in a good work to see those that follow follow with a good will.'

Clarke ends the chapter with a string of reflections the other witnesses would own. However God honoured Moses, Aaron, and Joshua, he ever taught the people to trust in himself alone, for 'what was even Moses without his God?' It is striking that at Moses' death despair did not overwhelm the camp: 'he who gave them Moses, has now given them Joshua in his place,' for when God has a great work to accomplish he will provide himself suitable instruments, and is not confined to any one. And though God promised his help so absolutely, 'yet he strongly insists on their own co-operation': even Joshua must be strong and very courageous, and the people must obey, or they would never possess the rest. So Clarke presses the chapter's last lesson home with the apostle's words — God works in us both to will and to do, 'therefore work out your own salvation with fear and trembling' (Philippians 2:12–13): it is enough that God gives the power; it is our duty to use it.
